topic: SHA-256 Recycling — Crypto Envelopes Around Known Answers (new section in Part 6)
our_argument_summary: Section argues that the dome's SHA-256 cryptographic hashing of predictions is technically competent but substantively meaningless — the content being hashed is overwhelmingly post-hoc (88% of crypto entries). Lists 7 April 4 batch entries restating WINs, claims 15 total restates_win, and argues that wrapping known answers in stronger crypto wrappers doesn't make them predictions. Also addresses OTS gap for WINs 035-039.

holes_found.description: FACTUAL ERROR: Hardcoded count '15 restates_win entries' is wrong. Actual count in predictions.json is 26. The section says 'Fifteen of {{PRED_TOTAL}} catalog entries explicitly restate existing WINs' but the data shows 26 entries with restates_win set. This is 30% of the 86 original entries (or 28% of the current 94), not the ~17% implied by 'Fifteen of 86.' A dome defender fact-checking this claim against our own published data/predictions.json would immediately find the discrepancy and use it to discredit the entire section's quantitative analysis. This error was present from the EXP-068 integration — the analyst miscounted.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'Fifteen' with the correct count (26) or, better, compute it dynamically via a template variable like {{RESTATES_WIN_COUNT}}. Also update the sentence to reflect that 26/94 = 28% of the catalog restates existing WINs — this is actually a STRONGER argument than the original 15/86 = 17%, so fixing the error strengthens our case.

holes_found.description: STALE HARDCODED NUMBER: 'the catalog lists 86 entries spanning 10 domains' — actual values are 94 entries spanning 12 categories. The '86' is hardcoded text not using the {{PRED_TOTAL}} template variable (which correctly resolves elsewhere in the same section). The '10 domains' is also wrong — predictions.json has 12 distinct categories (eclipse, geomagnetic, schumann, geodetic, coordinate, optical, aetheric_current, seismic, tidal, thermal, cosmological, toroidal). Two new categories (toroidal, thermal) were added when later waves of predictions were classified. This is the exact kind of brittle hardcoding we criticize the dome model for.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '86 entries' with '{{PRED_TOTAL}} entries' (or compute at build time). Replace '10 domains' with '{{PRED_CATEGORIES}} categories' or compute from data. The irony of hardcoding a count in a section about a model that hardcodes values should not be lost on us.

holes_found.description: UNDERSTATED RECYCLING IN APRIL 4 BATCH: Section says 'Of the 18 crypto-hashed entries in the April 4 batch, 7 directly restate already-confirmed WINs' and enumerates PRED-094, PRED-099, PRED-100, PRED-090, PRED-082, PRED-097, PRED-095. But the April 4 batch actually has 17 entries with restates_win set (out of 32 total in the batch). The 10 omitted restatements include PRED-TIER-2 (WIN-011), PRED-091 (WIN-066), PRED-TESLA-001 (WIN-062), PRED-TESLA-002 (WIN-001), PRED-TESLA-004 (WIN-002), PRED-TOROID-002 (WIN-053), PRED-TOROID-003 (WIN-038), PRED-TOROID-004 (WIN-053), PRED-TOROID-005 (WIN-053), PRED-TOROID-007 (WIN-053). The text only lists the PRED-0xx series while ignoring the PRED-TESLA, PRED-TOROID, and other namespaced entries. This dramatically understates the recycling — 17/32 = 53% of the April 4 batch restates WINs, not 7/18 = 39% of the crypto subset.
holes_found.recommendation: Expand the April 4 analysis: '17 of the 32 entries (53%) directly restate already-confirmed WINs.' Either enumerate all 17 or group them by category (e.g., '7 from the PRED-0xx series, 3 from PRED-TESLA, 5 from PRED-TOROID, plus PRED-TIER-2 and PRED-091'). The stronger number makes the recycling argument far more compelling.

holes_found.description: DOME DEFENSE NOT FULLY ADDRESSED: The dome's raw predictions page says '9 predictions confirmed before data came in' and lists specific WINs 035-039 as prospective confirmations. Our section addresses WINs 035-039 OTS but doesn't directly confront the '9 prospective' claim. A dome defender would ask: 'You only addressed 5 of my 9 prospective confirmations — what about the other 4?' The section should either enumerate all 9 or explain why 035-039 are the subset that matters.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence addressing the dome's '9 prospective' claim head-on, either by listing all 9 and showing why each fails our Section 3.1b standard, or by explaining that the remaining 4 are covered elsewhere in the review.

holes_found.description: POTENTIAL STRAWMAN ON GIT IMMUTABILITY: Section says 'git commit timestamps are independently verifiable via GitHub's server-side metadata.' This is more generous than necessary — git commit timestamps are author-set and trivially fakeable. Only GitHub's server-side push timestamp provides independent verification, and even that only proves when the commit was pushed, not when it was authored. Our section should be more precise here because the dome author could later claim we validated his git timestamps as independently verifiable when they're not.
holes_found.recommendation: Distinguish between git commit timestamps (author-settable) and GitHub server-side push timestamps (independently verifiable). This is actually a STRONGER argument — the crypto infrastructure the dome built is less secure than our section implies, which reinforces the conclusion that the envelope doesn't matter as much as the content.

holes_found.description: SECTION 3.1b REFERENCE AMBIGUITY: The section references 'our Section 3.1b post-hoc standard' and 'Section 3.1b' but the HTML anchor uses 'post-hoc-standard' as the onclick target. Need to verify this anchor exists and resolves to the correct section. If the reader clicks it and lands on the wrong section, the cross-reference undermines the argument's credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the anchor #post-hoc-standard exists in the built HTML and links to Section 3.1b (which is in the 'wins' tab). The onclick handler shows showTab('wins') which is correct for Part 3, but Section 3.1b is a subsection — confirm it scrolls to the right place.

holes_found.description: STRUCTURAL: Section buries the strongest stat. The most devastating number is that 53% of the April 4 batch restates WINs (if corrected per the major finding above), or that 26/94 = 28% of the entire catalog is recycled. Currently the section front-loads the crypto competence acknowledgment and buries the recycling stats in the middle. A motivated skeptic reading only the first paragraph gets: 'genuine methodological improvement... real engineering effort.' The killer stats come paragraphs later.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider leading with the recycling percentage, then acknowledging the crypto competence. 'More than a quarter of the dome's predictions catalog restates already-confirmed WINs. The crypto infrastructure wrapping these entries is technically competent — which makes the recycling pattern more striking, not less.'

stronger_arguments.description: The section misses the WIN-053 clustering problem. Five PRED-TOROID entries (002, 003, 004, 005, 007) all restate WIN-053 (two-pole geomagnetic model). This means a single WIN generates 5 separate predictions in the catalog. This is the most extreme case of inflation: one observation, one WIN, five 'predictions.' If the section highlighted this specific example, it would make the recycling argument visceral and concrete.

The section doesn't address whether the dome author's SHA-256 hashes are actually verifiable. Are the hashes published alongside the prediction text? Can an independent verifier recompute the hash from the prediction content and confirm it matches? If the hash is of a file that includes metadata (timestamps, version numbers), then the hash chain has a reproducibility gap that's worth noting.

kernel_of_truth.description: The section correctly acknowledges that the dome author has built genuine cryptographic provenance infrastructure — SHA-256 hashes in a public git repo, OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring, and a formal prediction registry. This IS more methodological rigor than most alternative cosmologies attempt, and the section is right to credit it before criticizing the content. The 'envelope vs. content' framing is intellectually honest and avoids the strawman of dismissing the infrastructure itself.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The section's core argument is sound: timestamping a known answer proves registration date, not prediction date. No amount of cryptographic sophistication changes the fact that 88% of hashed entries are post-hoc and 28% of the entire catalog restates existing WINs. The dome defender's best response — 'my git timestamps prove temporal priority by my own definition' — is addressed by the section's distinction between the dome's definition and our stricter Section 3.1b standard.

rewrite_scope: Fix the three factual errors (15→26 restates, 86→94/{{PRED_TOTAL}}, 10→12 categories), expand the April 4 batch analysis from 7 to 17 restatements, add the WIN-053 quintuple-restatement example, and restructure to lead with the strongest stats rather than burying them.

text_fingerprint.h3_title: SHA-256 Recycling — Crypto Envelopes Around Known Answers

citations_verified: Section 3.1b post-hoc standard (internal cross-reference — exists in part3 section)
Timestamp Error tab (internal cross-reference — exists as #timestamp-error anchor)
citations_unchecked: JPL D-22027 (referenced in broader OTS discussion but not in this specific section)

deeper_analysis: This section is the most data-dense addition to Part 6 since the restructure, and its core argument (crypto envelopes around known answers) is compelling and well-structured. The 'envelope vs content' framing is the right analytical lens. However, the section has three factual errors that are all in the SAME direction — they understate the recycling problem. The actual numbers are worse than what we report: 26 restates not 15, 17 April 4 batch restates not 7, 12 categories not 10. This is unusual — typically our errors overstate our case. Here we've understated it, which means fixing these errors actually strengthens the section. A dome defender doing a close read would catch the 15-vs-26 discrepancy first and potentially dismiss the entire quantitative analysis as sloppy, even though our qualitative argument is correct. The section should also anticipate the dome defender's strongest response: 'Your Section 3.1b standard is so strict that no prediction by anyone with domain knowledge could ever qualify. By that standard, even Einstein's perihelion calculation was post-hoc because he knew Mercury's orbital data.' Our text handles this implicitly (by distinguishing between the dome's definition and ours) but should make it explicit, because it's the most intellectually interesting objection and addressing it demonstrates rigor.
